content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission
April 11,
1994
Natalie Blanning, Treasurer
Rogan for Assembly
—
1994 and
Rogan for Assembly—Special Account
3401 Ocean View Blvd.
Glendale,
CA
91209
Re:
Your Request for Advice
Our File No.
A—94-113
Dear Ms. Blanning:
You have requested advice concerning the campaign provisions
of the Political Reform Act
(the
tIActII)1/
OUESTION
You are the campaign treasurer for James E.
Rogan,
who is
running for the vacant 43rd Assembly District seat in both the
May 3,
1994, special election and the regular state primary
election to be held June 7,
1994.
You have asked how campaign
expenses which benefit both campaigns,
such as salaries, office
expenses,
and consulting fees,
should be allocated between the
special election committee and the regular election committee.
CONCLUSION
campaign expenditures made by a candidate running in both a
special election and a regular election for the same office are
generally presumed to be in connection with the special election
and must be paid from the special election account.
However,
where expenditures are clearly being made for both campaigns,
the
candidate may use any reasonable method to allocate expenditures
between the two accounts.
Records should be maintained which
support the method of allocation.
Government Code Sections 81000—91015.
All statutory
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission regulations appear at
2 California Code of Regulations
Section 18000, et
All references to regulations are to Title
2,
Division
6 of the California Code of Regulations.
42S j Street. Suite SO()
•
P.O. Box S07
•
Sacranwnto, CA
95804—0S07
•
(916) 322-5660


Natalie Blanning
Our File No.
A—94—1l3
Page
2
FACTS
A special election will be held on May 3,
1994, to fill a
vacancy in the 43rd Assembly District.
That seat will also be
voted upon in the June 7,
1994, regular state primary election.
James E.
Rogan is a candidate in both elections and has
established a separate campaign bank account and controlled
committee for each election.
ANALYSIS
Candidates must establish a separate campaign bank account
and committee for each state or local elective office for which
they intend to solicit contributions or make expenditures.
(Section 85201; Regulation 18521.)
In addition, candidates
running in a special election to fill a vacant elective office are
subject to contribution limits under the Act.
(Section 85305.)
Regulation 18535(e)
requires expenditures made during the
special election cycle or the special runoff election cycle to be
made from the special election account “unless it is clear from
the surrounding circumstances that the expenditure is unrelated to
the special or special runoff election.”
2
!
In circumstances where a candidate is running in both a
special election and a regular election for the same office, it is
reasonable to assume that certain expenses,
such as office rent,.
staff salaries and consulting fees,
are attributab1eto both
campaigns.
Therefore,
you may allocate these expenses using any
reasonable method.
The committees should maintain records which
support the allocation.
If you have questions concerning this letter, please contact
met at
(916)
322—5662.
Sincerely,
Steven G.
Churchwell
General Counsel
By:
Carla Wardlow
Division Chief
Technical Assistance Division
2/
The “special election cycle” begins the day the office
becomes vacant and ends the day of the special election,
and the
“special runoff election cycle” begins the day after the special
election and ends the day of the special runoff election.
(Section 85305(b).)
